title: SASE Tunnel Health
app: `Splunk_TA_zscaler`, `Splunk_TA_paloalto` (Prisma Access), Cato Networks Events App (Splunkbase 8037), `TA-fortinet_fortigate` (FortiSASE), Netskope Add-on for Splunk (Splunkbase 3808)
spl:
index=sase sourcetype="sase:tunnel" earliest=-24h
| eval healthy=if(match(lower(state),"(?i)up|active") AND packet_loss_pct < 2 AND latency_ms < 200,1,0)
| where healthy=0
| stats latest(latency_ms) as latency_ms latest(packet_loss_pct) as loss by tunnel_id, site
| sort loss
description: Monitors IPSec/GRE/SSL tunnels from branch to SASE PoPs — packet loss, latency, and down events.
value: Network operations teams monitor SASE tunnel health across all sites and users, detecting down tunnels, degraded connections with high latency or packet loss, and flapping tunnels that silently degrade user experience without triggering traditional outage alerts.
implementation: Field names vary (Zscaler GRE, Prisma IPSec). Use unified summary index if multi-vendor.
visualization: Table (unhealthy tunnels), Geo map (site), Line chart (loss trend).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Authentication.Authentication
  by Authentication.user, Authentication.action, Authentication.src span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• SASE platform tunnel/connectivity logs. Zscaler: `sourcetype=zscaler:zia:tunnel` or `sourcetype=zscaler:zpa:connector`. Cato Networks: `sourcetype=cato:sdwan:events`. Palo Alto Prisma Access: `sourcetype=pan:globalprotect` for agent tunnels. Key fields vary: `tunnel_status` (up/down), `tunnel_type` (GRE/IPSec/DTLS), `site_name`, `bandwidth_mbps`, `latency_ms`, `packet_loss_pct`.
• SASE tunnels connect users (agent tunnel) and sites (IPSec/GRE) to the SASE cloud. Tunnel health directly affects user experience — a down tunnel means no access to cloud-delivered security services.
• Create a `sase_tunnels.csv` lookup: `tunnel_id,site_name,tunnel_type,expected_bandwidth_mbps,sla_latency_ms`.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify tunnel events:
```spl
index=sase earliest=-24h
| where isnotnull(tunnel_status)
| stats count by tunnel_status, tunnel_type
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Tunnel health overview:
```spl
index=sase earliest=-24h
| dedup tunnel_id sortby -_time
| lookup sase_tunnels.csv tunnel_id OUTPUT site_name expected_bandwidth_mbps sla_latency_ms
| eval health=case(
    tunnel_status="down", "DOWN",
    tonumber(latency_ms) > tonumber(sla_latency_ms)*2, "DEGRADED",
    tonumber(packet_loss_pct) > 2, "DEGRADED",
    1==1, "Healthy"
  )
| stats count by health, site_name, tunnel_type
| sort health
```

Understanding this SPL: Classifies each tunnel as Healthy, Degraded (high latency or packet loss), or Down. Degraded tunnels are worse than down tunnels in some ways because users experience slow and unreliable access but cannot easily tell why. Down tunnels trigger immediate failover (if configured); degraded tunnels just silently suffer.

Tunnel flapping:
```spl
index=sase earliest=-24h
| where tunnel_status="down" OR tunnel_status="up"
| stats count by tunnel_id, site_name
| where count > 10
| sort - count
```

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Check SASE admin console for tunnel status. Compare with Splunk.
(b) Disconnect a test tunnel and verify the down event.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — Tunnel status map. Row 2 — Degraded tunnels. Row 3 — Flapping tunnels.
Alert: Immediate on any site tunnel down. Warning on degraded (>SLA latency).

Runbook:
1. **Tunnel down** — Check ISP link at the site. Failover to backup tunnel if available.
2. **Degraded (high latency)** — ISP quality issue. Run traceroute from the site.
3. **Flapping** — Unstable ISP link or SASE PoP issue. Contact SASE vendor support.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No tunnel events** — SASE platform may not export tunnel status logs. Check vendor documentation.
• **`latency_ms` always 0** — Platform may not measure tunnel latency. Use external monitoring (ThousandEyes).
grandmaExplanation: All our internet security goes through cloud tunnels. If a tunnel breaks, people lose access to everything. We monitor every tunnel for problems — not just whether it is up or down, but also whether it is slow or unstable — because a limping tunnel is sometimes worse than one that is completely down.
